citations.narrative:
The facility housing two hyenas and various other mammals was not enclosed by an 8' tall perimeter fence in the following
areas:
- The fence along the road, on the west side of the facility, has been removed and construction is being done for a new
fence leaving the area without a perimeter fence.
- There was a section of fencing on the northwest side where a tree had fallen across the fence from the outside making
the fence approximately 5' high for about 6-8'. (Corrected at the time of inspection.)
- There was a 10' gate north of the bear enclosure with a one foot gap under about half the gate. (Corrected at the time of
inspection.)
- The 10' gate had a gap where it should have been latched to the perimeter fence (the opposite side from the hinges) that
was approximately 1' wide. The gate was leaning at the top away from the perimeter fence. (Corrected at the time of
inspection.)
An incomplete and inadequate perimeter fence can allow animals and unauthorized persons to enter the facility and have
contact with the animals.
All outdoor housing facilities must be enclosed by a perimeter fence that is of sufficient height to keep animals and
unauthorized persons out. Fences less than 8 feet high for potentially dangerous animals must be approved in writing by
the Administrator. The fence must be constructed so that it protects the animals in the facility by restricting animals and
unauthorized persons from going through it or under it and having contact with the animals in the facility, and so that it can
function as a secondary containment system for the animals in the facility.
To be corrected by June 1, 2026.
This inspection and exit interview were conducted with the licensee.
Additional Inspectors:
Ronald Richardson, VETERINARY MEDICAL OFFICER n

narrative:
3.127(d) 
   
Facilities, outdoor. 
The facility housing two hyenas and various other mammals was not enclosed by an 8' tall perimeter fence in the following 
areas: 
- The fence along the road, on the west side of the facility, has been removed and construction is being done for a new 
fence leaving the area without a perimeter fence. 
- There was a section of fencing on the northwest side where a tree had fallen across the fence from the outside making 
the fence approximately 5' high for about 6-8'. (Corrected at the time of inspection.) 
- There was a 10' gate north of the bear enclosure with a one foot gap under about half the gate. (Corrected at the time of 
inspection.) 
- The 10' gate had a gap where it should have been latched to the perimeter fence (the opposite side from the hinges) that 
was approximately 1' wide. The gate was leaning at the top away from the perimeter fence. (Corrected at the time of 
inspection.) 
 
An incomplete and inadequate perimeter fence can allow animals and unauthorized persons to enter the facility and have 
contact with the animals. 
 
All outdoor housing facilities must be enclosed by a perimeter fence that is of sufficient height to keep animals and 
unauthorized persons out. Fences less than 8 feet high for potentially dangerous animals must be approved in writing by 
the Administrator. The fence must be constructed so that it protects the animals in the facility by restricting animals and 
unauthorized persons from going through it or under it and having contact with the animals in the facility, and so that it can 
function as a secondary containment system for the animals in the facility. 
 
To be corrected by June 1, 2026. 
 
This inspection and exit interview were conducted with the licensee.

Additional Inspectors: 
Ronald Richardson, VETERINARY MEDICAL OFFICER n
